prompt: Batch 4 found only ranges for co-signer cost and NO named firms. Go deeper: identify SPECIFIC, NAMED firms and marketplaces that will supply a credentialed human to co-sign a third-party AI-agent security readiness attestation in 2026, with each one's PUBLISHED or quoted day-rate / hourly rate / fixed engagement fee AND typical turnaround time. Cover: named boutique vCISO firms (e.g. Cynomi-partner shops, Fractional CISO LLC, SideChannel, CISO Global, Vciso.com, Sentinel), expert networks (GLG, Catalant, Guidepoint, Dialectica) day-rates for a CISSP/CISA security expert, and named SOC2/pen-test firms that sell standalone readiness letters (Schellman, Prescient Assurance, Sprinto, Vanta auditor network, Johanson, A-LIGN). For EACH named firm give: the actual quoted number (URL or @handle+date), what the engagement includes, and turnaround in business days. Conclude with a build-vs-buy roster of the 3 most viable named co-signer suppliers for kolm's $25,000 Reviewed Attestation, with margin math at their real rates. Flag confidence per firm; say explicitly where a number is estimated vs. quoted verbatim.

Batch 4 could not find a named enterprise security questionnaire with verbatim AI-agent line-items. Go to PRIMARY sources and pull the ACTUAL line-item wording. Examine the latest published versions of: the Shared Assessments SIG (2025/2026, including any AI/SIG-AI module), the Cloud Security Alliance CAIQ / AI Controls Matrix (AICM), the Vendor Security Alliance VSA-Core/VSA-Full questionnaire, Google VSAQ, the HECVAT (higher-ed), and any Whistic/SafeBase/OneTrust standard AI questionnaire template. Quote the EXACT text of every question that concerns AI/agent/LLM behavior, model logging, prompt-injection defenses, autonomous-action controls, or AI sub-processor disclosure (verbatim, with document name + version + date + URL). Then map each quoted line-item to which one a kolm Ed25519-signed Readiness Report could let a vendor answer 'yes, see attached evidence'. Output a table: questionnaire | exact question text | can kolm satisfy it (full/partial/no) | what artifact proves it. Flag confidence; note where you are inferring wording vs. quoting it.

Pull the ACTUAL pricing-page numbers (verbatim, with URL + capture date) for kolm's nearest competitors as of mid-2026, not ranges. For EACH of: Cyphrex, Vanta (AI Trust / AI Security Assessment / Questionnaire Automation add-ons), Drata, SafeBase, Whistic, Conveyor, Secureframe, Sprinto, Lakera Guard, Prompt Security, and any 'AI trust center' product. Capture: every listed tier name, its exact monthly/annual/one-time price (or 'contact sales' if gated), seat/usage limits, and what is included. Where a price is gated, find any leaked/reported number (G2, Vendr, Spendflow, Reddit, @handle+date) and label it 'reported not official'. Then place kolm's locked ladder (free Scan; $750 one-time signed Readiness Report; $299/$999 mo Continuous; $25,000 flat Reviewed Attestation; +$10,000 Deep Red-Team) on a single comparison grid against these real figures, and identify the 3 sharpest pricing-page contrasts kolm can claim. Flag confidence per number; distinguish official vs reported.

Batch 4's beachhead pick rested on inference (confidence ~35). Build the ICP firmographics from PRIMARY data instead. For AI-native startups whose deals stall in enterprise security review, determine with cited evidence: (a) the company-size band (headcount, ARR) at which a first enterprise security questionnaire typically hits; (b) the funding stage (seed / A / B) and the typical raise size at which they start selling $100k+ enterprise deals; (c) the exact JOB TITLE that owns buying a security-readiness / attestation artifact (founder/CEO, Head of Security, GRC/Compliance lead, Head of Sales, Solutions/Sales Engineering) and at what headcount that role first appears; (d) how many such AI-native startups exist (TAM count) using Crunchbase/PitchBook/YC batch counts. Cite each with URL or @handle+date. Conclude with a one-paragraph ICP definition (sells X, stage Y, headcount Z, buyer-title W) and your confidence; flag inference vs. data.

Provide REAL per-channel CAC and sales-cycle benchmarks for selling a sub-$1,000 founder-led B2B security/compliance product to AI startups in 2026, with numbers not adjectives. For each channel give blended CAC (or cost-per-customer), typical sales-cycle length in days, and the source: (1) self-serve / PLG card-swipe via website; (2) founder cold outbound (email/LinkedIn/X DM); (3) YC/accelerator batch + founder Slack warm intro; (4) partner/marketplace listing (Vanta, Drata, MCP registry, AWS/GCP marketplace); (5) content/SEO inbound; (6) community (CISO Slacks, AI-founder X). Cite comparable companies' disclosed numbers (Vanta, Snyk, Sprinto, Pylon, early SOC2-automation tools) with URL/@handle+date. Then compute: at $750 one-time, which channels are CAC-positive and which are underwater, and what payback the $299/$999 mo continuous tier needs to rescue an underwater channel. Rank channels by CAC-efficiency for the first 100 customers; flag confidence and where CAC is estimated.

Batch 4 flagged $750 as possibly 'too cheap to trust' but had no controlled evidence. Find REAL price-anchor and A/B evidence for one-time professional/attestation deliverables in the $300-$5,000 band, 2026. Look for: published price-test results or willingness-to-pay studies for security readiness assessments, gap analyses, audit-readiness letters, or trust reports; Van Westendorp data points for one-time security deliverables; founder write-ups of having tested $X vs $Y for a compliance product and the conversion delta; any evidence that a higher anchor INCREASED trust/conversion for an attestation-type product. Quote verbatim (@handle+date or URL) with the actual conversion numbers where present. Then assess the locked $750 anchor: does evidence suggest $750 converts BETTER or WORSE than $1,500/$2,500 for a SIGNED report, and is there a 'too cheap to trust' threshold? Record any contradiction with the locked $750 as a FLAG for user approval (do not recommend silently changing it). Flag confidence; distinguish hard A/B data from anecdote.

Document the first-100-customer GO-TO-MARKET motion of comparable TRUST / ATTESTATION / compliance-proof products, with specific tactics and numbers. For each of Vanta, Drata, Secureframe, SafeBase, Sprinto, Oneleet, Delve, and any AI-trust-center startup: how did they get their first ~100 paying customers (channel, ICP, founder-led vs sales-led), how long did it take, what was the wedge price, and what single move inflected growth. Pull cited specifics: First Round / Lenny's / SaaStr interviews, founder X threads, YC pages (@handle+date or URL). Extract the repeatable PLAYBOOK steps that apply to kolm's $750 wedge into AI-native startups, ranked by transferability. Explicitly note where a tactic depended on conditions kolm lacks (timing, capital, integrations). Flag confidence per company; say which stories are well-documented vs. thin.

Pin down the EXACT EU AI Act and GPAI obligation dates and WHICH BUYER ROLE owns the compliance budget, from primary EU sources. From artificialintelligenceact.eu, the official EUR-Lex text, and the EU AI Office, list verbatim with article numbers and dates: (a) the GPAI provider obligations effective date(s) and any Code of Practice deadlines; (b) the high-risk system obligations effective date (Aug 2 2026 vs Aug 2 2027 distinction by Annex), including Article 12 logging; (c) penalty amounts and effective dates. Then, separately, determine from 2026 buyer-side sources WHICH internal role/budget line funds AI Act readiness spend (Chief Compliance Officer, CISO, DPO, General Counsel, Head of AI Governance) and the typical budget size, with cited evidence (@handle+date or URL). Conclude: which single date is the sharpest budget-unlock for a kolm-style readiness product and which job title to address urgency copy to. Cite every date; flag any uncertainty about scope or whether a date has shifted.

Get VERBATIM, NAMED-COMPANY proof of the core pain, which batch 4 lacked. Search 2025-2026 X and public write-ups for specific AI-native founders or operators (with @handle/name + date) describing, in their own words: an enterprise deal that stalled or died in a SECURITY/VENDOR review, the dollar size of that deal, how long the review took, and what artifact would have unblocked it. Prioritize agentic-coding, AI-SOC/security, fintech-AI, and healthcare-AI sellers at seed/Series-A. Quote each post verbatim and attribute it. Also capture any 2026 survey stat on % of AI startups losing/delaying deals to security review and median review duration (cited). Output: a ranked list of the 8 strongest verbatim pain quotes (most specific deal-size + timeline first) usable as evidence and as website social proof. Flag confidence; if verbatim named-company evidence remains thin, say so explicitly and rank by what little exists.

Benchmark the LIABILITY scaffolding and insurance cost a named co-signer needs, with real 2026 numbers, building past batch 4's qualitative answer. Find: (a) actual annual premium ranges for E&O / professional-liability insurance covering a security auditor / vCISO issuing third-party attestations (named carriers/brokers, quoted $ ranges, URL or @handle+date); (b) the specific scope-limiting and disclaimer language real attestation/readiness letters use to cap liability (quote sample 'readiness not certification' or limitation-of-liability clauses verbatim from public reports/templates); (c) whether errors-and-omissions claims against auditors for AI/security sign-offs have actually occurred (cite cases). Conclude with the minimum, costed legal+insurance package (insurance premium $/yr, indemnification terms, scope language) kolm must offer to recruit a named co-signer at the $25,000 tier, and how that cost loads into margin. Flag confidence; distinguish quoted premiums from estimates.

Quantify the AI/LLM/agent red-team and pentest market price points precisely to validate kolm's +$10,000 Deep Red-Team add-on, with real 2026 figures. Pull named-vendor quotes/pricing for: LLM/agent red-teaming and AI pentests from firms like Lakera, Robust Intelligence/Cisco AI Defense, HiddenLayer, Bishop Fox, NCC Group, Bugcrowd/HackerOne AI bounties, Cobalt, Prompt Security, and CredShields. For each give the actual quoted price or reported range, scope (number of agents/prompts, days of effort), and turnaround, cited (URL or @handle+date). Determine where a $10,000 fixed add-on sits: is it under-priced (leaving margin/credibility on the table), market, or over-priced vs. these named offerings? Record any contradiction with the locked +$10,000 as a FLAG for user approval, do not silently change it. Flag confidence; mark official vs reported prices.

Resolve the AUDIT BLOCKER on free-to-paid conversion: batch 4 found zero company-specific rates. Search harder for ANY disclosed or credibly-reported free-to-paid conversion number, time-lag, and trigger for bottoms-up SECURITY/DEVTOOL products with a free scan or free trust artifact: Snyk, Socket.dev, Semgrep, Aikido, Vanta free tools/Trust Center, Oneleet, Delve, SafeBase free trust pages, Wiz, Orca free scans. Pull from earnings calls, S-1/IPO filings, investor decks, podcast interviews, founder threads (URL or @handle+date) with the actual %. If still none exist, find the closest validated proxy (PLG security-tool freemium conversion from named SaaS benchmark reports with the source). Then model kolm's funnel: free Scan -> $750 report. At a 2%, 4%, and 8% free->paid rate, how many free scans are needed for the first 100 paying reports, and what does that imply for the channels in question l5? Flag confidence; clearly separate disclosed company rates from industry-aggregate proxies.

Identify the precise CONVERSION TRIGGER EVENT and its timing for buying a security-readiness/attestation artifact, with dated primary evidence beyond batch 4. Find 2026 verbatim accounts (@handle+date or URL) of the exact moment a buyer paid: (a) an enterprise reviewer explicitly demanded AI-agent evidence; (b) a named deal with a close-date deadline; (c) a board/investor security ask; (d) a regulatory deadline (EU AI Act); (e) a security incident. For each trigger, quote the source and note how fast they bought (same-day card swipe vs weeks). Rank the triggers by (frequency x urgency) for AI-native startups, and specify the exact free-Scan gating and in-product moment kolm should use to catch each trigger (e.g. show a failing finding tied to a named questionnaire line-item, then offer the $750 signed report). Output concrete before/after website/funnel copy suggestions tied to the top 2 triggers, queued for later approval. Flag confidence; mark inferred vs. quoted.

Map the REVIEWER-SIDE acceptance path with named, current surfaces and the exact mechanic that gets a new attestation format reused, deeper than batch 4. Identify the specific 2026 trust-exchange / questionnaire-automation networks where a kolm attestation could be uploaded and auto-matched to buyer questions: SafeBase Trust Center, Whistic Profile/Exchange, Conveyor (formerly Loopio security), Vanta Trust Center, Drata Trust Center, OneTrust, UpGuard, Shared Assessments TPRM exchange. For each: does it allow a vendor to attach a third-party signed report/evidence file, can buyers auto-map it to SIG/CAIQ answers, and is there a partner/marketplace listing path (cite URL or @handle+date, and any integration/API docs). Then state the single most credible 'distribution-by-attachment' move: where kolm reports get seen by reviewers with least friction, and what would make a reviewer accept and REUSE a kolm attestation rather than re-asking. Rank the surfaces; flag confidence and any access barriers.

Stress-test the $999/mo -> $25,000 gap and the overall ladder against 2026 stated willingness-to-pay, with dollar-specific evidence (extending batch 4's w1). Find verbatim 2026 evidence (@handle+date or URL) of what AI startups actually pay or refuse for: continuous compliance monitoring at the $300-$1,000/mo band; one-off reviewed/named attestations at $10k-$30k; and whether buyers perceive a dangerous gap between a self-serve $999/mo product and a $25,000 human-reviewed tier. Probe specifically: is an intermediate tier (e.g. a lighter human-reviewed report between $999/mo and $25k) something buyers ask for, and at what price? Pull any competitor's actual mid-tier pricing that bridges self-serve to enterprise (cite the number). Conclude with a verdict on the gap (hold the ladder as locked / flag a missing intermediate tier) recorded as a FLAG for user approval, not a silent change. Give dollar evidence per point and confidence; mark official vs reported.

Compile the procurement/legal artifacts a kolm attestation must align with so it is ACCEPTED, not just produced, with primary-source specifics. Identify the standard vendor-onboarding documents enterprise procurement requires from a small AI vendor in 2026 and which a kolm Ed25519-signed Readiness Report can satisfy or accompany: the security questionnaire (covered in l2), plus the DPA/sub-processor list, the AI/model-use addendum, the vendor risk assessment / TPRM scorecard, SOC2/ISO mapping letters, and any 'AI vendor' rider now appearing in MSAs. Quote real 2026 examples of AI-specific procurement clauses or 'AI vendor security requirements' wording (URL or @handle+date), including any from regulated buyers (finance/health/gov). For each artifact, state whether kolm should (a) generate it, (b) map its report to it, or (c) stay out of scope. Conclude with the minimum artifact set kolm's report must reference to clear a typical enterprise procurement gate. Flag confidence; distinguish quoted clauses from inferred requirements.
